caption.text: Throughout the last century, East Boston has been the home of numerous city housing, development, and neighborhood revitalization projects. Before the first public housing, Maverick Gardens, was developed in the early 1940s. The project was originally known as the "East Boston public housing project," and can be seen outlined in red.
caption.title: Introduction

caption.text: In 1940, Boston [gained the funding](https://www.proquest.com/docview/816891146/E2548D8D3BB647BEPQ/4?accountid=12826&sourcetype=Newspapers) to begin work on the East Boston public housing project. The project would be located between New St., Maverick St., Havre St., and Sumner St. Its primary aim would be to house the families of soldiers fighting in World War II, but would [also include](https://www.proquest.com/docview/839320215/E2548D8D3BB647BEPQ/6?sourcetype=Newspapers) some general low income housing.

The housing project looked very different from the homes there before. Previously, [as shown in this newspaper clip](https://www.proquest.com/docview/816891146/E2548D8D3BB647BEPQ/4?sourcetype=Newspapers), the buildings ranged in appearance and height, crafted with wood paneling, some having porches and others having gabled roofs.

All of the buildings in the new project were three stories high, made of brick, and exactly uniform, spanning several blocks. These buildings stuck out in their surroundings, and some of the previous individuality and neighborhood charm was lost in new redevelopment.

What kind of neighborhood would the housing project replace? Long before the housing project, East Boston was the city's major industrial site. This map shows that industry on the waterfront was already prevalent here, serving as a shipyard for imports and the start of the railroad to ship the products across the state. By 1922, just 20 years before the public housing would begin construction, East Boston was already booming with industries like coal, wool, electricity, and storage.

East Boston residents had ample employment opportunites right on their doorstep. This photo depicts Sumner Street before the public housing development. On the right side, the many buildings lining the street housed families and working class Bostonians.
Just across the street sits Staples Coal Company...
...and just a block away at the intersection of New Street is Boston Cold Storage Company.
The neighborhood blended together with the factories and plants, meaning that these residents likely lived here for their proximity to work.
The industrial neighborhoods along the waterfront were also well-connected to the rest of Boston, thanks to the bustling trolley station in Maverick Square.

This lively image taken in 1918 shows Maverick Square as a gathering space, waiting for the trolley pictured in the center to arrive. It would have been convenient for residents here, who only had to step outside to reach their train station.
Maverick Square was also home to the Maverick House, a luxury hotel built in 1835.

The Maverick House was not only a hotel marketed towards out-of-towners, but also a place for the community to come together and enjoy a game of billiards, cigars, or the luxury bar. College students used the hotel as a space to meet up and relax after classes. It was demolished in 1924 to make room for a new train station.

The East Boston housing project would primarily target families, meaning that the nearby area needed to provide adequate schooling for the children. Maverick Square's close proximity to schools may be one of the reasons this particular location was chosen.
The closest school to the housing project was Fitton School, located on Havre Street and only a block away from some parts of the housing project

Fitton School was a Catholic school that [opened its doors to East Boston](https://www.proquest.com/docview/497371487/19F7D844D6574040PQ/1?accountid=12826&sourcetype=Newspapers) in 1893. The school was named after Father Fitton, who was the pastor of the only Catholic Church in East Boston in 1855.

Located right next to the Catholic Church of the Holy Redeemer and the Notre Dame Convent, the Catholic church was a central aspect of the school’s operation and education. The school was quite small at its opening, [handing out only seven diplomas](https://www.proquest.com/docview/499341402/E1959FFF475C4D0CPQ/5?accountid=12826&sourcetype=Newspapers) in 1900.
The school remained open until 1974, when it became East Boston Central Catholic school…
…And still utilizes the original building today.
Residents of the East Boston public housing project had numerous nearby churches to choose from. Besides the Catholic Church of the Holy Redeemer, nearby religious buildings included...
The Union Methodist Episcopal Church...
Maverick Congregational Church...
...and the Swedish Lutheran Church. This variety of churches shows that while East Boston was predominantly Christian and Catholic, residents practiced a diversity of denominations.

The population of East Boston itself was so diverse largely due to its function as an immigration port. The East Boston Immigration Station was Boston’s [first intentionally created](ails/eastbostonimmigr00bost/page/4/mode/2up) immigration entrance, opening in 1920.

Rather than functioning like Ellis Island, which required all migrants to enter through the facility, the East Boston Immigration Station served as a place to bring migrants who did not instantly pass their medical check or needed further documentation review. Many immigrants even expressed dissatisfaction with the immigration center, one calling it [“more like a jail”](https://archive.org/details/eastbostonimmigr00bost/page/20/mode/2up) than a station.

East Bostonians welcomed immigrants to their neighborhood with open arms. Erected in 1912, [The Immigrants Home](https://globalboston.bc.edu/index.php/home/immigrant-places/east-boston/) was built and run by Protestants to house migrant mothers and children, located just across the street from the Immigration Station.

Italian immigrants made up a majority of the East Boston population, even before the migration center opened. Many of the households displaced by the Maverick Gardens project as you can see here – Giannasca, Brignoli, Carvotta, Paolillo, to name a few – were Italian names. The housing project would disrupt the cultural community already established in East Boston.

Italian migrant families also had larger-than-average households - the average Italian household in the early 1900s [held twelve residents,](https://globalboston.bc.edu/index.php/home/immigrant-places/east-boston/italians-in-east-boston/) meaning that the preexisting homes in the neighborhood were designed with larger families in mind.

The limited number of schools in East Boston, plus the above-average number of schoolchildren living in the neighborhood, proved a roadblock for Maverick Gardens residents after the project's development. In 1953, [it was announced](https://www.proquest.com/docview/840191076/7FEA99992424FE5PQ/4?accountid=12826&sourcetype=Newspapers) that the basement of the East Boston housing project would become a classroom, due to “the nearest schools [being] filled to capacity.” The same article noted that nearly 200 children across two housing projects – including East Boston – were unable to attend school for this reason.

With over 64,000 residents, the population of East Boston [peaked in 1925](https://globalboston.bc.edu/index.php/home/immigrant-places/east-boston/) - only 20 years before the public housing project would take effect. The Italian migrant population would begin to decline after World War II, coinciding with the housing development's completion. While many factors likely impacted this, such as young people moving away from their hometown, the changing landscape brought with the project may have been one reason.

The East Boston Relief center was the area's primary healthcare center and was very close to the planned housing project, but the Maverick Gardens construction coincided with ongoing controversy surrounding the hospital.

Only a few years before the housing project’s construction began, Boston’s mayor, Mayor Tobin, [announced his plan](https://www.proquest.com/docview/817119213/12117EB251544B72PQ/1?sourcetype=Newspapers) to close the Boston Relief Center. This announcement resulted in public outcry.

Community members, even pastors and priests, in East Boston [urged one another to protest](https://www.proquest.com/docview/817123855/71F784168AB34B52PQ/2?accountid=12826&sourcetype=Newspapers), denouncing the closure, but despite the backlash, the center closed before reopening with shorter hours. Seven years later in 1945 - once the public housing had been constructed for several years -  the Mayor [reconsidered restoring](https://www.proquest.com/docview/839855374/BF668B6EA9404C0APQ/8?accountid=12826&sourcetype=Newspapers) the center’s 24 hour opening status. This shows that readily available healthcare for Maverick Gardens residents was never a guarantee, especially in its first few years of operation.

The housing project continued to implement changes that East Bostonians were vocal about. In 1956, “about 100 residents of the East Boston Housing Project” conducted a march on City Hall to demand that proposed rent increases be halted. [Even Massachusetts representatives](https://www.proquest.com/docview/843352631/80A6E96CD88E418FPQ/4?accountid=12826&sourcetype=Newspapers) weighed in on the matter; Rico Matera told renters that if there wasn’t at least a six month delay in the increases, “don’t pay that rent increase, and there’s not a judge in East Boston that would convict you.” The increase in rent for public housing meant that the surrounding neighborhoods were also being priced out of their homes, too.

Some parts of the public housing project, though, did bring the community together. The East Boston housing project was [one of the first communities](https://www.proquest.com/docview/817155630/7FEA99992424FE5PQ/6?accountid=12826&sourcetype=Newspapers) to receive a city victory garden during World War II. We have no photograph of the specific garden; however, one can imagine it growing in the courtyard between the buildings, or in one of the grassy lots beneath the first-floor windows. Working together on the garden would have helped the housing project feel more communal and garner a sense of deeper patriotism. Perhaps it even connected other neighbors in East Boston to the new residents, to ease some of the distance or unhappiness caused by the construction encroaching on their neighborhood.
While the Maverick Gardens public housing project no longer exists as originally built, it laid the groundwork for future East Boston housing projects, which are still continuing today.

As the city continues to “revitalize” East Boston, many residents still face displacement and uncertainty just as they did in 1941. In 2018, radio station ZUMIX chronicled community stories and memories of East Boston residents in a project titled Constelación de Historias. To hear these stories, you can watch the videos [here.](https://vimeo.com/user90033856)
